title: False promise to marry as rape - how courts test 'consent'
p1:
A promise to marry can change the meaning of consent in law. But does every broken promise become a criminal offence?

Many assume that if a relationship ends after a marriage promise, it automatically means consent was obtained through deception.
p2:
The legal test is more nuanced. Courts do not only ask whether the marriage promise was later fulfilled. They examine whether the promise was false from the beginning and whether it was made with no intention of being honoured.

A surprising point: a failed relationship and a dishonest promise are not always treated the same way.
p3:
The key question is the state of mind at the time consent was given. Courts look at facts, circumstances, and evidence to decide whether consent was genuine or obtained through deception.

The practical takeaway: the law focuses less on the outcome of the relationship and more on whether there was dishonest intent from the start.
conclusion: Should the law draw a clearer line between a genuine relationship that fails and consent obtained through a false promise to marry?
